title: Uterine Rupture During a Trial of Labor After Cesarean (TOLAC/VBAC) in a 32-Year-Old G3P2 on Oxytocin Augmentation: Recognizing Sudden Severe Abdominal Pain + Abrupt Fetal Bradycardia + Loss of Fetal Station as an Obstetric Catastrophe, Stopping Oxytocin and Resuscitating the Mother, and LEADING the Emergency Team to an Immediate Laparotomy/Cesarean
subtitle: UTERINE RUPTURE = a complete, full-thickness disruption of the uterine wall (often through the serosa) during pregnancy or labor, through which the fetus, placenta, and/or umbilical cord may extrude into the peritoneal cavity — abruptly cutting off fetal oxygenation and producing rapid intra-abdominal MATERNAL HEMORRHAGE. It is one of the most catastrophic obstetric emergencies, threatening BOTH the mother (hemorrhagic shock, DIC, death) and the fetus (acute hypoxia, neurologic injury, death), and outcomes are decided in minutes by RECOGNITION and a fast, coordinated team response. It must be distinguished from a UTERINE SCAR DEHISCENCE — an incomplete separation of a prior scar with the visceral peritoneum still intact, usually asymptomatic and often found incidentally — whereas a COMPLETE rupture is symptomatic and life-threatening. RISK FACTORS center on a WEAKENED or OVERSTRESSED uterus: a PRIOR CESAREAN or uterine scar is the leading risk (a classical/vertical incision carries the highest risk; a prior low-transverse incision is lower but NOT zero — the setting of a TRIAL OF LABOR AFTER CESAREAN, TOLAC/VBAC), other uterine surgery (e.g., myomectomy), EXCESSIVE OXYTOCIN or uterine TACHYSYSTOLE/hyperstimulation, induction of a scarred uterus with PROSTAGLANDINS, grand multiparity, a short interpregnancy interval, obstructed labor/cephalopelvic disproportion, fetal macrosomia, malpresentation, external cephalic version, and abdominal TRAUMA. RECOGNITION (high-yield cues): the MOST COMMON and often EARLIEST sign is an ABRUPT, ABNORMAL FETAL HEART RATE pattern — a sudden PROLONGED FETAL BRADYCARDIA or new recurrent late/variable decelerations, or loss of the FHR; classic maternal signs include SUDDEN, SEVERE, CONSTANT abdominal/uterine PAIN (sometimes described as a 'tearing' or 'ripping' sensation, occasionally a 'pop' that may transiently feel like relief), LOSS OF FETAL STATION (the presenting part recedes/rises and is no longer palpable at the previously documented station as the fetus extrudes upward), CESSATION or sudden change in the uterine contraction pattern / loss of uterine tone, PALPABLE FETAL PARTS through the maternal abdomen, a change in abdominal contour, and signs of MATERNAL HEMORRHAGE/HYPOVOLEMIC SHOCK (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, restlessness, air hunger) — note that vaginal bleeding may be present, scant, or CONCEALED because much of the bleeding is intra-abdominal. IMMEDIATE MANAGEMENT — the goals are to STOP the precipitant, RESUSCITATE the mother, and EXPEDITE DELIVERY (the only definitive treatment): (1) RECOGNIZE the catastrophe and CALL FOR HELP / activate the obstetric emergency (rapid-response) team instantly — mobilize the obstetrician/surgeon, anesthesia, the neonatal/NICU team, the OR, the BLOOD BANK, and the charge nurse — do not manage it alone or leave the patient; (2) STOP the OXYTOCIN immediately (it intensifies the contractions and worsens the rupture/bleeding); (3) RESUSCITATE the mother for hemorrhagic shock: high-flow OXYGEN, TWO large-bore IVs, rapid IV crystalloid, send labs (CBC, coagulation studies/fibrinogen, type & crossmatch), and ACTIVATE THE MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL — anticipate transfusion of packed red cells and clotting products and watch for DIC; (4) maintain CONTINUOUS fetal heart rate monitoring and optimize maternal perfusion while preparing for surgery (definitive relief is delivery, not intrauterine resuscitation alone); (5) EXPEDITE TRANSFER TO THE OR for an IMMEDIATE (STAT) LAPAROTOMY → emergency CESAREAN delivery and HEMORRHAGE CONTROL (the surgeon performs uterine REPAIR if feasible or a HYSTERECTOMY if bleeding is uncontrolled), minimizing the decision-to-incision interval; (6) have the NEONATAL team present and ready for RESUSCITATION (NRP) because the fetus is likely hypoxic/acidotic; insert a Foley, and DOCUMENT the timeline. LEADERSHIP / DELEGATION / TEAMWORK are decisive and are the focus of this case: the nurse must instantly RECOGNIZE the emergency, CALL IT OUT and activate the team, ASSIGN CLEAR ROLES with CLOSED-LOOP COMMUNICATION (one person stops the oxytocin and runs fluids/resuscitation and stays with the patient; others notify/mobilize the surgeon + anesthesia + neonatal team + OR + blood bank, obtain/expedite consent and surgical prep, draw labs and start blood, monitor maternal and fetal status, support the terrified family, and document times), hand off with SBAR, and DELEGATE BY SCOPE (unlicensed/support staff fetch equipment, transport, set up the OR, and run blood; licensed staff perform assessment, medication administration, and FHR interpretation). PREVENTION/AWARENESS: select TOLAC candidates carefully (favor prior low-transverse incisions, avoid in classical/high-vertical scars), use oxytocin judiciously and AVOID tachysystole, avoid prostaglandin cervical ripening of a scarred uterus, monitor the laboring VBAC patient closely with continuous FHR, and maintain heightened vigilance for the cues above. NURSING ROLE: recognize uterine rupture the instant the fetal heart rate drops and the mother reports sudden severe pain, STOP the oxytocin, lead and delegate the coordinated emergency response, resuscitate the mother for hemorrhagic shock (with the massive transfusion protocol), and expedite a STAT laparotomy/cesarean with the neonatal team ready — all while supporting the frightened mother and family.

patient.name: L. Navarro
patient.code: Full code; laboring patient + fetus (two patients); attempting TOLAC after one prior low-transverse cesarean; partner at bedside; goal = instantly recognize uterine rupture, stop oxytocin, activate and lead the emergency team, resuscitate the mother for hemorrhagic shock, and expedite a STAT laparotomy/cesarean to save mother and fetus
patient.admitDate: Admitted in active labor at term (G3P2) attempting a trial of labor after cesarean (TOLAC/VBAC) after one prior LOW-TRANSVERSE cesarean; oxytocin augmentation in progress. At approximately 7 cm dilated she SUDDENLY developed severe, constant abdominal pain with a 'tearing' sensation, an ABRUPT PROLONGED FETAL BRADYCARDIA (FHR ~70), LOSS OF FETAL STATION (the presenting part receded), cessation of the contraction pattern, palpable fetal parts through the abdomen, and rising maternal tachycardia/hypotension — UTERINE RUPTURE, an obstetric catastrophe. The nurse must recognize it instantly, stop the oxytocin, lead the coordinated emergency response, resuscitate the mother, and expedite a STAT laparotomy/cesarean.
patient.room: Labor & Delivery; continuous electronic fetal monitoring; OR + anesthesia + neonatal/NICU team + blood bank being mobilized; partner at bedside; obstetric emergency response activated

vitals.time: Onset — sudden severe pain during TOLAC
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; sudden SEVERE, CONSTANT abdominal pain with a 'tearing' sensation during oxytocin augmentation, accompanied by an ABRUPT PROLONGED FETAL BRADYCARDIA (FHR ~70 bpm); abrupt severe pain + fetal bradycardia in a TOLAC patient is uterine rupture until proven otherwise

vitals.time: Recognition — loss of station + shock signs
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; the presenting part has RECEDED (loss of fetal station), the uterine contraction pattern has CEASED/lost tone, fetal parts are palpable through the abdomen, and the mother is tachycardic, hypotensive, pale, and restless = hemorrhage/hypovolemic shock (bleeding largely intra-abdominal/concealed); FHR remains bradycardic = OVERT UTERINE RUPTURE

37.0°C; OXYTOCIN STOPPED immediately; high-flow oxygen; TWO large-bore IVs with rapid crystalloid; labs sent (CBC, coags/fibrinogen, type & cross) and MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL activated; continuous maternal + fetal monitoring; obstetric emergency team activated (OB/surgeon, anesthesia, neonatal/NICU, OR, blood bank, charge nurse) with closed-loop role assignment; expediting to the OR

vitals.time: Transfer to OR for STAT laparotomy/cesarean
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; rapid transfer to the OR with decision-to-incision minimized; blood products en route/started; emergency consent and surgical prep underway; neonatal team present and ready for NRP; the only definitive treatment is immediate surgical delivery + hemorrhage control

37.0°C; emergent laparotomy/cesarean completed; NEWBORN required PPV per NRP then improved (Apgars ~3 → 7 → 8), cord gases reflecting acute compromise but improving; uterine rupture REPAIRED (hysterectomy on standby for uncontrolled bleeding); hemorrhage controlled; transfused per MTP; maternal hemodynamics stabilizing

37.0°C; mother hemodynamically stable post-op, hemoglobin stabilized after transfusion, monitored for ongoing bleeding/DIC and postpartum hemorrhage; newborn in NICU as indicated; team DEBRIEF conducted (rapid recognition, oxytocin stopped, coordinated resuscitation + expedited delivery); emotional support for the family; documentation of the timeline complete

labs.v: 32 y/o woman L. Navarro, G3P2 at term, attempting a TRIAL OF LABOR AFTER CESAREAN (TOLAC/VBAC) after one prior LOW-TRANSVERSE cesarean, on OXYTOCIN augmentation. At ~7 cm she SUDDENLY developed severe, constant abdominal pain with a 'tearing' sensation, immediately accompanied by an ABRUPT PROLONGED FETAL BRADYCARDIA (FHR ~70). On assessment the presenting part had RECEDED (LOSS OF FETAL STATION), the contraction pattern had ceased/lost tone, fetal parts were palpable through the abdomen, and she became tachycardic, hypotensive, pale, and restless — signs of hemorrhagic shock. This constellation in a scarred uterus = UTERINE RUPTURE, a complete full-thickness disruption of the uterine wall through which the fetus/placenta may extrude into the peritoneal cavity, abruptly impairing fetal oxygenation and causing intra-abdominal maternal hemorrhage (vaginal bleeding may be scant/concealed). It is distinct from a uterine scar DEHISCENCE (incomplete, peritoneum intact, usually asymptomatic). RISK FACTORS present: a prior cesarean/uterine scar (the leading risk — the TOLAC setting) plus oxytocin augmentation. OB/PMH: G3P2, one prior low-transverse cesarean, otherwise uncomplicated term pregnancy. SOCIAL: partner at bedside, frightened. The fetus is at acute risk of hypoxic injury and the mother of exsanguination — minutes matter. Immediate priorities: STOP the oxytocin, activate and LEAD the emergency team, resuscitate the mother, and expedite a STAT laparotomy/cesarean — all while supporting the terrified family. This is both a clinical emergency and a LEADERSHIP/DELEGATION challenge requiring instant recognition, calling for help, and coordinated role assignment.
labs.r: Sudden severe constant abdominal pain ('tearing') + abrupt PROLONGED FETAL BRADYCARDIA + LOSS OF STATION + cessation of contractions + palpable fetal parts + maternal hemorrhagic-shock signs, in a scarred uterus (TOLAC) = UTERINE RUPTURE until proven otherwise (vaginal bleeding may be concealed/intra-abdominal). Abnormal FHR is the most common/earliest sign. Complete rupture (catastrophic) vs scar dehiscence (incomplete, peritoneum intact, asymptomatic). Risk: prior cesarean/scar (leading), excessive oxytocin/tachysystole, prostaglandin induction of a scar. RN role: instant recognition + stop oxytocin + activate/lead the team + resuscitate + expedite delivery.

labs.n: Immediate interventions — stop oxytocin, resuscitate, expedite delivery
labs.v: The IMMEDIATE goals in uterine rupture are to STOP the precipitant, RESUSCITATE the mother, and EXPEDITE DELIVERY (the only definitive treatment): (1) RECOGNIZE the catastrophe and CALL FOR HELP / ACTIVATE THE OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY TEAM instantly — summon the obstetrician/surgeon, anesthesia, the neonatal/NICU team, the OR, the BLOOD BANK, and the charge nurse; do not leave the patient or manage it alone. (2) STOP THE OXYTOCIN immediately — ongoing contractions intensify the rupture and bleeding; a tocolytic is generally NOT the answer (delivery is). (3) RESUSCITATE the mother for hemorrhagic shock: apply high-flow OXYGEN, establish TWO LARGE-BORE IVs, give rapid IV crystalloid, send labs (CBC, COAGULATION studies/FIBRINOGEN, type & crossmatch), and ACTIVATE THE MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL — anticipate packed red cells plus clotting products and watch for DIC; insert a Foley and monitor urine output. (4) Maintain CONTINUOUS fetal heart rate monitoring and optimize maternal perfusion (positioning, fluids) while preparing for surgery — but understand that intrauterine resuscitation alone will NOT fix this; delivery is the cure. (5) EXPEDITE TRANSFER TO THE OR for an IMMEDIATE (STAT) LAPAROTOMY → emergency CESAREAN delivery and HEMORRHAGE CONTROL, minimizing the decision-to-incision interval; the surgeon performs uterine REPAIR if feasible or a HYSTERECTOMY if bleeding is uncontrolled. (6) Ensure the NEONATAL team is present and ready for RESUSCITATION (NRP) because the fetus is likely hypoxic/acidotic; obtain cord gases. The single most important sequence the moment rupture is identified is to STOP the oxytocin, MOBILIZE the team, RESUSCITATE the mother, and GET TO THE OR — fast.
labs.r: Stop the precipitant + resuscitate + expedite delivery: (1) CALL FOR HELP / activate the team (OB/surgeon, anesthesia, neonatal, OR, blood bank); (2) STOP OXYTOCIN immediately; (3) resuscitate hemorrhagic shock — high-flow O2, TWO large-bore IVs, rapid crystalloid, labs (CBC/coags/fibrinogen/type&cross), ACTIVATE MASSIVE TRANSFUSION, Foley/UOP, watch for DIC; (4) continuous FHR + optimize perfusion (delivery is the cure, not intrauterine resuscitation alone); (5) STAT laparotomy → cesarean + hemorrhage control (repair vs hysterectomy), minimize decision-to-incision; (6) neonatal team ready for NRP + cord gases. RN role: stop oxytocin, resuscitate, get to the OR fast.

labs.v: Uterine rupture is a paradigm 'call it out and run the room' emergency, and outcomes hinge on LEADERSHIP, DELEGATION, and TEAMWORK. The responding nurse must: (1) RECOGNIZE the emergency instantly (sudden severe pain + abrupt fetal bradycardia + loss of station in a TOLAC patient) and immediately CALL FOR HELP / activate the obstetric rapid-response team rather than managing it alone; (2) ASSIGN CLEAR ROLES with CLOSED-LOOP COMMUNICATION as people arrive, because many time-critical tasks must happen simultaneously: ONE clinician STOPS the oxytocin, starts/runs the fluids and resuscitation, and stays with the patient; others NOTIFY/mobilize the SURGEON, ANESTHESIA, the NEONATAL/NICU team, the OR, and the BLOOD BANK; draw labs and START BLOOD per the massive transfusion protocol; obtain/expedite emergency CONSENT and surgical prep; MONITOR maternal and fetal status; SUPPORT the frightened patient/partner; and DOCUMENT the timeline (time of symptom onset, FHR change, recognition, oxytocin stopped, interventions, decision-to-incision, delivery). (3) Communicate using SBAR for concise handoffs to each responder and to the OR/anesthesia/neonatal teams (Situation: suspected uterine rupture with fetal bradycardia and maternal shock; Background: G3P2, TOLAC after low-transverse cesarean, on oxytocin; Assessment: sudden severe pain, loss of station, palpable fetal parts, hypotension/tachycardia; Recommendation: STAT laparotomy/cesarean, activate MTP, neonatal team and OR ready). (4) DELEGATE BY SCOPE: unlicensed/support staff fetch equipment, transport the patient, set up the OR, and run blood from the bank, while RN/provider-level tasks (assessment, medication administration, FHR interpretation, blood administration per policy) remain with licensed staff. (5) Maintain SITUATIONAL leadership — keep the team focused, prevent duplicated/missed tasks, and move the patient to the OR rapidly (the decision-to-incision interval matters). (6) After the emergency, LEAD a DEBRIEF (what went well, opportunities) and support the team and family. This coordinated, role-clear, closed-loop response — built on instant recognition, stopping the oxytocin, and rapid resuscitation — is what gets a salvageable mother and fetus to delivery in time.
labs.r: Leadership/delegation in uterine rupture: instantly recognize + CALL FOR HELP/activate the team (don't manage alone); assign clear roles with CLOSED-LOOP communication (one person stops oxytocin + runs resuscitation + stays with the patient; others mobilize surgeon/anesthesia/neonatal/OR/blood bank, draw labs + start blood per MTP, expedite consent/prep, monitor mother + fetus, support family, document times); SBAR handoffs; delegate by scope (UAP fetch/transport/OR setup/run blood; licensed = assessment/meds/FHR/blood admin); debrief after. RN role: lead + delegate the coordinated response.

labs.n: Prevention/awareness, neonatal care, and Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: PREVENTION/AWARENESS: uterine rupture cannot always be prevented, but risk is reduced and detection hastened by — careful TOLAC candidate SELECTION (favor a prior LOW-TRANSVERSE incision; AVOID a trial of labor with a prior classical/high-vertical or T-shaped incision or prior rupture); JUDICIOUS oxytocin use with AVOIDANCE of TACHYSYSTOLE/hyperstimulation; AVOIDING prostaglandin cervical ripening of a scarred uterus; CONTINUOUS fetal heart rate monitoring during a VBAC (the FHR is the early-warning system); ready availability of an OR, anesthesia, and blood for any TOLAC; and heightened VIGILANCE for the warning cues. NEONATAL CARE: because the fetus endures acute hypoxia/possible acidosis, the NEONATAL team must be present and ready for RESUSCITATION (NRP — warm/dry/stimulate, assess, positive-pressure ventilation, and escalation as needed), and cord blood gases are obtained; outcomes are best when rupture is recognized immediately and delivery is expedited. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct emergency practice): the nurse did NOT dismiss the sudden severe pain and fetal bradycardia as 'normal labor'; did NOT continue or increase the oxytocin (it was STOPPED); did NOT encourage continued pushing for a vaginal birth despite loss of station; did NOT delay for a routine ultrasound when the clinical picture was clear; did NOT leave the patient to go find help alone (called for help while staying + resuscitating); and did NOT withhold activation of the massive transfusion protocol/large-bore access. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: instant RECOGNITION of the cue cluster as rupture; immediately STOPPING the oxytocin; rapid maternal RESUSCITATION (O2, two large-bore IVs, fluids, MTP) with anticipation of DIC; rapid activation + coordinated LEADERSHIP of the emergency team with clear role delegation + SBAR; an expedited STAT laparotomy/cesarean with hemorrhage control (repair or hysterectomy); the neonatal team ready for NRP; and supportive care + debrief. EXPECTED outcome (as here): with rapid recognition, oxytocin stopped, brisk resuscitation, and an expedited surgical delivery, the mother's hemorrhage is controlled and stabilized and the newborn — though it may need resuscitation — does well. The case underscores that uterine rupture is won or lost in the first minutes by RECOGNIZING it, STOPPING the oxytocin, RESUSCITATING the mother, and LEADING a fast, coordinated team to the OR.
labs.r: Prevention/awareness: careful TOLAC selection (favor prior low-transverse; avoid classical/high-vertical scar or prior rupture), judicious oxytocin + avoid tachysystole, avoid prostaglandin ripening of a scar, CONTINUOUS FHR during VBAC (early warning), OR/anesthesia/blood ready. Neonatal team ready for NRP + cord gases. Negatives confirm correct practice (didn't call it normal labor, didn't continue/increase oxytocin, didn't push for vaginal birth, didn't delay for imaging, didn't leave the patient, didn't withhold MTP). Protective: instant recognition + stop oxytocin + rapid resuscitation/MTP + coordinated team leadership + STAT laparotomy/cesarean + neonatal readiness. RN role: recognize + stop oxytocin + resuscitate + lead → delivery.

labs.v: 7.1 g/dL (fell from a baseline of 11.8 with acute hemorrhage)
118 mg/dL (consumption — concerning for DIC; pregnancy fibrinogen is normally elevated, so this low value is especially worrisome)

nursesNote: Labor & Delivery — 32 y/o G3P2 at term attempting TOLAC/VBAC after one prior LOW-TRANSVERSE cesarean, on OXYTOCIN augmentation. At ~7 cm she SUDDENLY developed severe, constant abdominal pain ('tearing') with an ABRUPT PROLONGED FETAL BRADYCARDIA (FHR ~70). On assessment: LOSS OF FETAL STATION (presenting part receded), contraction pattern CEASED/lost tone, fetal parts palpable through the abdomen, and maternal tachycardia/hypotension/pallor/restlessness = hemorrhage/hypovolemic shock (bleeding largely intra-abdominal/concealed). In a scarred uterus this = UTERINE RUPTURE (complete full-thickness disruption; distinct from an asymptomatic scar dehiscence). Risk factors present: prior cesarean/scar (leading) + oxytocin augmentation. Abnormal FHR is the most common/earliest sign. Partner at bedside, frightened. IMMEDIATE RESPONSE (stop the precipitant + resuscitate + expedite delivery + LEAD the team): (1) CALLED FOR HELP / activated the obstetric emergency team (OB/surgeon, anesthesia, neonatal/NICU, OR, blood bank, charge nurse) WITHOUT leaving the patient; (2) STOPPED THE OXYTOCIN immediately; (3) resuscitated for hemorrhagic shock — high-flow O2 (SpO2 100%), TWO large-bore IVs with rapid crystalloid, labs sent (CBC, coags/FIBRINOGEN, type & cross), MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL activated, Foley placed, watching for DIC; (4) continuous maternal + fetal monitoring while preparing for surgery (delivery is the cure, not intrauterine resuscitation alone); (5) expedited STAT LAPAROTOMY/CESAREAN with decision-to-incision minimized; (6) neonatal team present + ready for NRP. LEADERSHIP/DELEGATION: assigned clear roles with CLOSED-LOOP communication — one clinician stopped oxytocin + ran resuscitation + stayed with the patient; others mobilized surgeon/anesthesia/neonatal/OR/blood bank, drew labs + started blood per MTP, expedited consent + surgical prep, monitored mother + fetus, supported the partner, and documented the timeline (onset, FHR change, recognition, oxytocin stopped, interventions, decision-to-incision, delivery); SBAR handoffs to OR/anesthesia/neonatal. DELIVERY: emergent laparotomy/cesarean completed; NEWBORN needed PPV per NRP then improved (Apgars ~3 → 7 → 8; cord gases reflecting acute compromise, improving); uterine rupture REPAIRED (hysterectomy on standby for uncontrolled bleeding); hemorrhage controlled; transfused per MTP; mother stabilizing. POSTOP: mother hemodynamically stable, Hgb stabilized after transfusion, monitored for ongoing bleeding/DIC + postpartum hemorrhage; newborn in NICU as indicated; team DEBRIEF (rapid recognition + oxytocin stopped + coordinated resuscitation + expedited delivery); emotional support for the family; documentation complete. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT call it normal labor, did NOT continue/increase oxytocin, did NOT push for vaginal birth, did NOT delay for routine imaging, did NOT leave the patient, did NOT withhold the MTP/large-bore access.
providerOrders: OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY: suspected uterine rupture during TOLAC with fetal bradycardia and maternal hemorrhagic shock — activate the obstetric rapid-response/emergency team immediately
DIAGNOSIS: uterine rupture (sudden severe pain, abrupt fetal bradycardia, loss of fetal station, palpable fetal parts, maternal shock) in a scarred uterus on oxytocin augmentation
CODE STATUS: full code; two patients (mother + fetus); expedite delivery
STOP the precipitant:
STOP the oxytocin infusion immediately
RESUSCITATE the mother (hemorrhagic shock):
High-flow oxygen; establish TWO large-bore IVs; rapid IV crystalloid bolus
STAT labs: CBC, coagulation studies/fibrinogen, type & crossmatch
Activate the MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL (packed red cells + clotting products); monitor for DIC
Insert an indwelling urinary catheter; monitor urine output; continuous maternal vital-sign and cardiac monitoring
Maintain continuous electronic fetal heart rate monitoring; optimize maternal positioning/perfusion while preparing for surgery
EXPEDITE DELIVERY (definitive treatment):
Prepare for and transfer to a STAT laparotomy → emergency cesarean delivery and hemorrhage control; minimize decision-to-incision time
Surgeon to perform uterine repair if feasible or hysterectomy if bleeding is uncontrolled; obtain emergency consent
TEAM LEADERSHIP / DELEGATION / COMMUNICATION:
Call for help / activate the team immediately; do not leave the patient or manage alone

Assign clear roles with closed-loop communication: one clinician stops oxytocin + runs resuscitation + stays with the patient; others mobilize surgeon/anesthesia/neonatal/OR/blood bank, draw labs + start blood, expedite consent/prep, monitor mother + fetus, support the family, and document times
SBAR handoffs to the OR, anesthesia, and neonatal teams; delegate by scope (UAP/support: fetch/transport/OR setup/run blood; licensed: assessment/meds/FHR interpretation/blood administration)
Document the timeline: symptom onset, FHR change, recognition, oxytocin stopped, interventions, decision-to-incision, and delivery
NEONATAL PREPARATION:
Neonatal/NICU team present and ready for resuscitation (NRP) at delivery; obtain cord blood gases
MATERNAL/FAMILY SUPPORT + AFTERCARE:
Support the frightened mother/partner with clear, calm communication throughout
Postoperative recovery care; monitor for ongoing bleeding/DIC and postpartum hemorrhage; neonatal assessment/NICU as indicated
Conduct a team debrief; complete documentation
PREVENTION/AWARENESS (general):

Careful TOLAC candidate selection (favor prior low-transverse incision; avoid trial of labor with a classical/high-vertical scar or prior rupture); judicious oxytocin with avoidance of tachysystole; avoid prostaglandin ripening of a scarred uterus; continuous FHR monitoring with OR/anesthesia/blood readily available during a VBAC

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this laboring TOLAC patient?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Sudden, severe, constant abdominal/uterine pain (often described as 'tearing,' sometimes a 'pop') during a trial of labor after cesarean
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — sudden severe pain in a scarred uterus is a classic warning of uterine rupture. RN role: recognize + act immediately.

opts.text: An abrupt, prolonged fetal bradycardia (or new recurrent late/variable decelerations, or loss of the FHR) — the most common, often earliest sign of rupture
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the fetal heart rate is the early-warning system; an abrupt prolonged bradycardia is the hallmark. RN role: recognize the FHR change.

opts.text: Loss of fetal station (the presenting part recedes/rises) and/or palpable fetal parts through the maternal abdomen
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — loss of station and palpable fetal parts suggest the fetus has extruded through the rupture. RN role: recognize + escalate.

opts.text: Cessation or sudden change in the uterine contraction pattern / loss of uterine tone
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — an abrupt change in contractions/tone is part of the rupture picture. RN role: recognize the pattern change.

opts.text: Maternal signs of hemorrhage/hypovolemic shock — tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, restlessness; vaginal bleeding may be scant or concealed (intra-abdominal)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — much of the bleeding is intra-abdominal, so shock can develop with little visible bleeding. RN role: recognize shock + resuscitate.

opts.text: Risk factors present — a prior cesarean/uterine scar (the leading risk) plus oxytocin augmentation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — risk-factor awareness heightens vigilance and explains the event. RN role: recognize the high-risk setup.

opts.text: The immediate priorities are to STOP the oxytocin, activate/lead the emergency team, resuscitate the mother, and expedite delivery (laparotomy/cesarean)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — stopping the precipitant + resuscitating + delivering is the response framework. RN role: stop oxytocin + lead + resuscitate + expedite.

opts.text: Reassure the patient that severe constant abdominal pain with a drop in the fetal heart rate is an expected part of active labor, and continue the oxytocin
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Sudden severe pain with an abrupt fetal bradycardia in a TOLAC patient signals rupture; the oxytocin must be stopped and the emergency team activated — never dismissed as normal labor.

opts.text: Encourage the mother to push harder and continue attempting a vaginal birth despite the loss of fetal station
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. With a likely rupture and loss of station, the path to delivery is a STAT laparotomy/cesarean — continued pushing worsens hemorrhage and fetal hypoxia.

opts.text: Uterine rupture is a full-thickness disruption of the uterine wall through which the fetus/placenta may extrude, causing rapid fetal hypoxia and maternal hemorrhage — an emergency where minutes matter
opts.rat: CORRECT — the pathophysiology + urgency. Foundation for the emergency response.

opts.text: An abrupt, prolonged fetal bradycardia is the most reliable and often earliest indicator of rupture
opts.rat: CORRECT — the FHR is the early-warning sign. Foundation for prompt recognition.

opts.text: A prior cesarean/uterine scar is the leading risk factor; excessive oxytocin/tachysystole and prostaglandin induction of a scarred uterus add risk
opts.rat: CORRECT — risk factors. Foundation for vigilance + prevention.

opts.text: A complete rupture (catastrophic, symptomatic) is distinct from a uterine scar dehiscence (incomplete separation, peritoneum intact, often asymptomatic/incidental)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the rupture-vs-dehiscence distinction. Foundation for understanding severity.

opts.text: The definitive treatment is an immediate laparotomy for delivery and hemorrhage control (uterine repair or hysterectomy) with maternal resuscitation — intrauterine resuscitation alone will not fix it
opts.rat: CORRECT — delivery is the cure. Foundation for expediting the OR.

opts.text: It is a team emergency: instant recognition + calling for help + stopping the oxytocin + closed-loop role assignment + activating massive transfusion drive the outcome
opts.rat: CORRECT — leadership/delegation principle. Foundation for a coordinated response.

opts.text: Uterine rupture can be safely managed expectantly with bed rest and serial ultrasounds while labor is allowed to continue
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. A complete rupture is a surgical emergency — expectant management would allow fatal maternal hemorrhage and fetal hypoxia; expedite delivery.

opts.text: Because the fetal heart rate may briefly recover, delivery can be deferred and contractions augmented to achieve a vaginal birth
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Augmenting contractions worsens the rupture and bleeding; once rupture is suspected, the response is to stop oxytocin and deliver surgically, not to pursue a vaginal birth.

opts.text: Recognize the rupture, STOP the oxytocin, and call for help / activate and lead the obstetric emergency team (do not leave the patient or manage alone)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Stopping the precipitant and mobilizing the team are the first, most time-critical actions and trigger everything else. RN role: recognize + stop oxytocin + activate/lead.

opts.text: Resuscitate the mother — high-flow O2, two large-bore IVs + rapid crystalloid, type & cross / activate massive transfusion, continuous maternal + fetal monitoring
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Aggressive resuscitation for hemorrhagic shock buys time and is done simultaneously with mobilizing the OR. RN role: O2/IV/fluids/MTP/monitor.

opts.text: Expedite transfer to the OR for an immediate (STAT) laparotomy/cesarean — the only definitive treatment
opts.rat: #3 — Definitive step. Delivery + hemorrhage control is the cure; minimize decision-to-incision. RN role: expedite the OR.

opts.text: Ready the neonatal team for resuscitation (NRP) and anticipate postpartum hemorrhage, possible hysterectomy, and DIC
opts.rat: #4 — Prepare for the consequences. The neonate is likely compromised and ongoing hemorrhage/coagulopathy must be anticipated. RN role: neonatal readiness + anticipate complications.

opts.text: Support the frightened mother/family and document the timeline; debrief afterward
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + after. Family support + documentation + debrief complete the response. RN role: support + document + debrief.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this uterine rupture.

opts.text: Stop the oxytocin infusion immediately
opts.rat: Ongoing contractions worsen the rupture and bleeding — stopping oxytocin is a first, essential action. RN role: stop the precipitant.

opts.text: Call for help / activate the obstetric emergency team and assign clear roles with closed-loop communication
opts.rat: Team activation + leadership are essential to a fast, coordinated response. RN role: activate + delegate.

opts.text: Apply high-flow oxygen, establish two large-bore IVs, give rapid IV crystalloid, and obtain type & crossmatch / activate the massive transfusion protocol
opts.rat: Aggressive resuscitation for hemorrhagic shock with blood readiness. RN role: resuscitate + activate MTP.

opts.text: Maintain continuous fetal heart rate monitoring and optimize maternal perfusion while preparing for surgery
opts.rat: Monitoring and perfusion support are appropriate while moving toward the definitive delivery. RN role: monitor + optimize perfusion.

opts.text: Expedite transfer to the OR for an immediate laparotomy/cesarean delivery with hemorrhage control (repair or hysterectomy)
opts.rat: Surgical delivery + hemorrhage control is the definitive treatment. RN role: expedite the OR.

opts.text: Ensure the neonatal team is present and ready for NRP, and anticipate postpartum hemorrhage, possible hysterectomy, and DIC
opts.rat: The neonate is likely hypoxic, and ongoing hemorrhage/coagulopathy must be anticipated. RN role: neonatal readiness + anticipate complications.

opts.text: Insert a Foley catheter, send labs (CBC, coagulation studies/fibrinogen, type & cross), and document the timeline
opts.rat: Supports monitoring, transfusion, and the legal/clinical record. RN role: Foley/labs/documentation.

opts.text: Continue or increase the oxytocin to strengthen contractions and achieve a faster vaginal delivery
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — oxytocin must be STOPPED; stronger contractions worsen the rupture and hemorrhage. The path to delivery is a STAT cesarean.

opts.text: Reassure the patient that the pain and fetal heart rate drop are normal labor and wait for a routine ultrasound before acting
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the clinical picture is a surgical emergency; dismissing it or delaying for imaging risks maternal and fetal death.

opts.text: Encourage the mother to push and continue attempting a vaginal birth despite loss of station and fetal bradycardia
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — with rupture and loss of station, continued pushing worsens hemorrhage/hypoxia; deliver by STAT laparotomy/cesarean.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Immediately STOP the oxytocin and call for help / activate the emergency team while beginning maternal resuscitation (without leaving the patient)
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Stopping the precipitant and summoning the team while starting resuscitation are the most time-critical actions, done together. RN role: stop oxytocin + call out + resuscitate.

opts.text: Resuscitate — high-flow O2, two large-bore IVs + rapid crystalloid, type & cross / activate massive transfusion, continuous maternal + fetal monitoring
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Aggressive resuscitation for hemorrhagic shock stabilizes the mother while preparing for delivery. RN role: O2/IV/fluids/MTP/monitor.

opts.text: Expedite transfer to the OR for a STAT laparotomy/cesarean with the neonatal team ready for NRP
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Move to the definitive treatment — surgical delivery + hemorrhage control — without delay. RN role: expedite the OR + neonatal readiness.

opts.text: Anticipate and manage hemorrhage (repair vs hysterectomy, transfusion, watch for DIC); insert a Foley and send labs
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Manage ongoing bleeding and coagulopathy and support monitoring/transfusion. RN role: hemorrhage management + labs/Foley.

opts.text: Support the family throughout, document the timeline, and debrief the team afterward
opts.rat: #5 — Throughout/after. Support + documentation + debrief complete the response. RN role: support + document + debrief.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: The nurse recognized the sudden pain + abrupt fetal bradycardia + loss of station as rupture, stopped the oxytocin, activated the team, resuscitated, and expedited to the OR — and the patient was delivered rapidly
opts.rat: Correct immediate response stopped the precipitant, resuscitated, and moved to definitive delivery. Model handling improving both patients' trajectory.

opts.text: Emergent laparotomy/cesarean delivered the newborn (brief PPV per NRP then vigorous, Apgars 3 → 7 → 8); the uterine rupture was repaired and hemorrhage controlled
opts.rat: Expedited definitive delivery with hemorrhage control. Correct trajectory toward a good outcome.

opts.text: With rapid crystalloid and transfusion per the massive transfusion protocol, the mother's tachycardia and hypotension resolved and her hemoglobin stabilized
opts.rat: Effective resuscitation reversing hemorrhagic shock. Expected favorable response.

opts.text: Postoperatively the mother is hemodynamically stable, monitored for ongoing bleeding/DIC, and the family was supported; the team debriefed
opts.rat: Resolution + monitoring + debrief + family support. Complete, appropriate handling.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the nurse attributed the severe pain and fetal bradycardia to normal labor and continued the oxytocin; the fetal status and maternal hemorrhage deteriorated during the delay
opts.rat: ⚠️ Dismissing the cues and continuing oxytocin allowed ongoing rupture, bleeding, and fetal hypoxia. Demonstrates why the cues are recognized and oxytocin stopped immediately.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — oxytocin was increased to hasten delivery and the mother was encouraged to push for a vaginal birth despite loss of station; bleeding and fetal hypoxia worsened
opts.rat: ⚠️ Augmenting contractions and pursuing a vaginal birth worsen the rupture and hemorrhage. Demonstrates the contraindicated actions in uterine rupture.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the massive transfusion protocol was not activated and large-bore access was delayed; the mother developed worsening hemorrhagic shock and coagulopathy
opts.rat: ⚠️ Delayed resuscitation/blood allowed shock and DIC to progress. Demonstrates the importance of early large-bore access and MTP activation.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the nurse left the patient to go find the physician and gather supplies alone, with no one resuscitating; the mother and fetus deteriorated during the delay
opts.rat: ⚠️ Leaving the patient and not resuscitating wasted critical minutes. Demonstrates calling for help WHILE staying, stopping oxytocin, and resuscitating.

opts.text: In the minutes immediately after recognition, with the oxytocin stopped and the team mobilizing the OR, the fetal heart rate remains bradycardic until delivery
opts.rat: Expected — the cause (rupture) is only corrected by delivery, so the FHR may stay bradycardic until the fetus is delivered. Maintain resuscitation and expedite the OR.

opts.text: Weeks later, a teaching review notes that judicious oxytocin use (avoiding tachysystole), careful TOLAC selection, and continuous FHR monitoring support earlier recognition and prevention of rupture
opts.rat: Prevention/awareness practice that improves future recognition and reduces risk. Reflects sound system learning.
